chapters.title: What is Python?
chapters.content:
Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language created by Guido van Rossum in 1991. It is famous for its readable, clean syntax — Python code often looks like plain English.

Python is used for:
  • Data science and machine learning (NumPy, Pandas, PyTorch)
  • Web development (Django, Flask, FastAPI)
  • Automation and scripting
  • Scientific computing
  • AI and natural language processing
  • Game development (Pygame)

Python is interpreted — no compilation step. Just run:
  python3 hello.py

Python enforces indentation — code blocks are defined by spaces, not curly braces.

chapters.title: Hello World & Basics
chapters.content:
print("Hello, world! 🌸")

That's it. No class, no main function, no semicolons.

Variables — no type declaration needed:
  name = "Alice"
  age  = 21
  pi   = 3.14159
  cool = True

Python figures out the type automatically.

Comments:
  # single line comment

  """
  multi-line comment
  or docstring
  """

Print with formatting:
  print(f"Hello {name}, you are {age}!")   # f-string
  print("Hi", name, "you are", age)         # comma-separated
  print("Value: %d" % 42)                   # old style

Input from user:
  name = input("What is your name? ")
  print(f"Hello {name}!")

chapters.title: Data Types
chapters.content:
Python's built-in types:

Numbers:
  x = 42          # int
  y = 3.14        # float
  z = 2 + 3j      # complex
  big = 10**100   # Python ints have no size limit!

Strings:
  s = "hello"
  s = 'hello'         # same thing
  s = """multi
  line"""             # multi-line
  len(s)              # length
  s.upper()           # HELLO
  s[0]                # h
  s[-1]               # last char: o
  s[1:3]              # slice: el
  s * 3               # hellohellohello

Booleans:
  True  False   (capital T and F!)
  not True == False
  True and False == False
  True or False == True

None:
  x = None    # like null in other languages

chapters.title: Lists, Tuples, Dicts, Sets
chapters.content:
List — ordered, mutable:
  nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
  nums.append(6)       # add to end
  nums.pop()           # remove last
  nums[0]              # first: 1
  nums[-1]             # last: 6
  nums[1:3]            # slice: [2, 3]
  len(nums)            # 5

Tuple — ordered, IMMUTABLE:
  point = (10, 20)
  x, y = point         # unpacking

Dictionary — key-value:
  person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 21}
  person["name"]       # Alice
  person["city"] = "Tokyo"    # add key
  person.keys()        # dict_keys
  person.values()      # dict_values
  person.items()       # dict_items
  "name" in person     # True

Set — unique unordered:
  s = {1, 2, 3, 2, 1}  # {1, 2, 3}
  s.add(4)
  s.union({5, 6})
  s.intersection({2, 3, 7})   # {2, 3}

List comprehension (very Pythonic):
  squares = [x**2 for x in range(10)]
  evens   = [x for x in nums if x % 2 == 0]

chapters.title: Control Flow
chapters.content:
If / elif / else:
  if age >= 18:
      print("adult")
  elif age >= 13:
      print("teen")
  else:
      print("child")

Note: NO parentheses needed, colon at end, indent the body.

For loop:
  for i in range(5):          # 0 1 2 3 4
      print(i)

  for item in ["a","b","c"]:
      print(item)

  for i, item in enumerate(lst):  # get index too
      print(i, item)

  for k, v in dict.items():
      print(k, v)

While loop:
  n = 0
  while n < 10:
      n += 1

Break and continue:
  for i in range(100):
      if i == 5: break     # stop loop
      if i % 2: continue   # skip odd

One-line if (ternary):
  label = "yes" if x > 0 else "no"

chapters.title: Functions
chapters.content:
Defining a function:
  def greet(name):
      return f"Hello, {name}! 🌸"

  print(greet("Alice"))

Default arguments:
  def power(base, exp=2):
      return base ** exp

  power(3)      # 9   (uses default exp=2)
  power(3, 3)   # 27

Keyword arguments:
  def connect(host, port=80, secure=False):
      ...

  connect("localhost", secure=True, port=443)

Args and kwargs:
  def add(*args):          # any number of args
      return sum(args)
  add(1, 2, 3, 4)   # 10

  def show(**kwargs):      # any keyword args
      for k, v in kwargs.items():
          print(k, v)
  show(name="Alice", age=21)

Lambda (anonymous function):
  square = lambda x: x**2
  sorted(names, key=lambda s: s.lower())

chapters.title: Classes & OOP
chapters.content:
class Dog:
    # class variable (shared by all instances)
    species = "Canis familiaris"

    def __init__(self, name, age):   # constructor
        self.name = name             # instance variable
        self.age  = age

    def __str__(self):               # string representation
        return f"Dog({self.name}, {self.age})"

    def bark(self):
        print(f"{self.name} says: Woof!")

d = Dog("Luna", 3)
d.bark()          # Luna says: Woof!
print(str(d))     # Dog(Luna, 3)

Inheritance:
  class Puppy(Dog):
      def __init__(self, name):
          super().__init__(name, 0)

      def bark(self):
          print(f"{self.name} says: Yip!")

Special (dunder) methods:
  __init__    constructor
  __str__     str(obj)
  __len__     len(obj)
  __eq__      obj == other
  __add__     obj + other

chapters.title: Useful Libraries
chapters.content:
Python's standard library:

os — file system:
  import os
  os.getcwd()              # current directory
  os.listdir('.')          # list files
  os.path.exists('file')   # check if file exists
  os.makedirs('dir')       # create directory

sys — system info:
  import sys
  sys.argv      # command line arguments
  sys.exit(0)   # exit program

json — data format:
  import json
  data = json.loads('{"x": 1}')     # parse string
  json.dumps({"x": 1})              # to string
  json.load(open('file.json'))      # from file

datetime:
  from datetime import datetime
  now = datetime.now()
  print(now.strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M"))

random:
  import random
  random.randint(1, 10)      # random int
  random.choice([1,2,3])     # random item
  random.shuffle(lst)         # shuffle in place

Pip — install packages:
  pip install requests
  pip install numpy pandas matplotlib
